g.data-narration: Welcome to Peterson's Solution - a fundamental software algorithm for achieving mutual exclusion in concurrent processes.
The critical section problem occurs when multiple processes access shared resources simultaneously, causing race conditions and data inconsistency.
g.text: What is the Critical Section Problem?

text.tspan: When multiple processes access shared resources (memory, files, printers) concurrently,
they can interfere with each other causing race conditions and data inconsistency.

g.data-narration: A valid solution must satisfy three requirements: mutual exclusion ensures only one process enters, progress allows entry when possible, and bounded waiting prevents starvation.

g.text: Only ONE process in critical section
No blocking when CS is free

g.data-narration: Peterson's solution uses two shared variables: flag array to indicate process interest, and turn variable to resolve conflicts fairly.
The entry section has three steps: declare interest by setting flag true, yield priority to the other process, then busy wait if needed.
g.text: Entry Section (Process i, where j = 1 - i)

g.data-narration: The exit section simply resets the flag to false, signaling that the process has left the critical section and others can now enter.

g.text: Other process can now exit busy-wait and enter CS

g.data-narration: Initially both flags are false and turn is zero, meaning neither process wants to enter and process zero has priority if there's contention.

text.tspan: No one wants to enter CS

g.data-narration: In sequential execution, process zero enters first because its flag is set while process one's flag remains false, allowing immediate entry.
When both processes try to enter simultaneously, the turn variable breaks the tie - whoever has turn value different from themselves waits.
g.text: Both processes attempt to enter at the same time

g.data-narration: Mutual exclusion is guaranteed because both processes cannot satisfy the while condition simultaneously - only one can have turn matching their own index.

text.tspan: Assume both P0 and P1 enter CS simultaneously.
For P0 to enter: flag[1]=False OR turn=0
For P1 to enter: flag[0]=False OR turn=1
But turn can only be 0 or 1 at a time!

g.data-narration: Progress is satisfied because when no process is in critical section and someone wants to enter, they can. Bounded waiting is guaranteed by the turn variable ensuring fairness.

text.tspan: If no process is in CS and some want to enter:
• That process will NOT wait (turn ≠ j)
• Each process gets turn at most once
• After waiting, process will enter CS

g.data-narration: Bounded waiting works because when process zero yields by setting turn to one, process one can enter, and after exiting, process zero gets its turn.
Peterson's solution has three key limitations: busy waiting wastes CPU cycles, it only works for two processes, and modern hardware provides better alternatives.
In summary, Peterson's solution is a classic software algorithm that achieves mutual exclusion using flag and turn variables, satisfying all three critical section requirements.
